skill.name: linux-privilege-escalation
skill.description: Guides authorized security testers through Linux privilege escalation enumeration, exploit path analysis, and remediation reporting.
skill.summary: Assess Linux systems for privilege escalation paths and document remediation steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are confirmed by semantic review: the skill is an operational Linux privilege escalation playbook, not only an audit checklist. It includes root shell objectives, password file extraction, SUID shell creation, reverse shells, and attacker-hosted payload workflows. No prompt injection text was found, but the offensive exploitation content warrants blocking.

critical_findings.title: Explicit Offensive Privilege Escalation Objective
critical_findings.description: The skill states that it enables escalation from a low-privilege user to root-level control and lists root shell access as a primary deliverable. This confirms that many static command findings are intended exploitation steps, not incidental examples.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The stated purpose and deliverables explicitly target root-level control. The surrounding workflow is a sequence of exploitation phases.

critical_findings.title: Credential and Password File Extraction
critical_findings.description: The skill instructs users to read passwd and shadow data, generate cracking inputs, and crack password hashes offline. This creates direct risk of credential theft and account compromise.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines show direct shadow file access and offline cracking workflow. Legitimate auditing is possible, but the steps are immediately usable for credential theft.

critical_findings.title: Root Persistence Through Account and SUID Modification
critical_findings.description: The skill provides steps to create SUID root shells and add a UID 0 account through password file modification. These actions alter system trust boundaries and can provide persistent privileged access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple independent sections describe privileged shell creation or UID 0 account insertion. These are concrete privilege-maintenance techniques.

critical_findings.title: Reverse Shell and Attacker Infrastructure Guidance
critical_findings.description: The skill requires attacker infrastructure and includes reverse shell one-liners plus cron-based shell callbacks. This can enable unauthorized remote control of a target host.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names an attacker machine and gives direct callback techniques. Placeholders reduce target specificity but not operational misuse risk.

high_findings.title: Sudo and Capability Shell Escape Recipes
high_findings.description: The skill documents sudo and Linux capability abuse patterns that spawn privileged shells. These are confirmed true positives for the static sudo and command execution findings.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit privileged shell escape examples. The context confirms they are intended to obtain root execution.

high_findings.title: Remote Script and Exploit Execution Workflow
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to pipe a downloaded enumeration script into a shell and to transfer, compile, and run exploit source on targets. This confirms the network and process execution findings as high risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are common in authorized testing, but they also execute downloaded or transferred code on a target. The exploit workflow makes the risk clear.

high_findings.title: Kernel Exploit Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill maps kernel versions to named exploits and instructs users to compile and execute exploit code. Failed kernel exploits can crash systems, and successful ones can grant root privileges.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is explicitly about finding, compiling, and executing kernel exploits. The skill itself acknowledges production crash risk.

medium_findings.title: Broad Reconnaissance in an Exploitation Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill includes system, process, network, cron, capability, and filesystem enumeration. These commands can be legitimate audit inputs, but here they support later exploitation phases.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Enumeration commands alone are often benign. Confidence is high because the same document chains them into exploit execution and root shell goals.

low_findings.title: Public Reference URLs Are Low Risk by Themselves
low_findings.description: Some hardcoded URLs point to public security resources rather than exfiltration endpoints. They are not independently malicious, although one download command is risky when piped to a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are public references to security tooling and documentation. The risk depends on surrounding execution instructions, which are captured in higher severity findings.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Matches Are Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick execution detections are Markdown formatting or shell code block boundaries, not Ruby execution in a runnable plugin file. They remain relevant as documentation examples, but the specific Ruby backtick pattern is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fenced or inline command examples, not executable Ruby source. This dismisses the specific static pattern while preserving the broader misuse assessment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Shell Escapes
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands use sudo, SUID, and Linux capabilities to spawn root shells.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly invoke privileged shells. The exploit context is explicit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Password File Abuse
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions read shadow data and add a root-equivalent account entry.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines cover password hash extraction, cracking preparation, and UID 0 account creation. These are direct account compromise techniques.

dangerous_patterns.title: SUID Shell Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill creates or modifies shell binaries with SUID permissions for elevated execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The same privilege-maintenance pattern appears in several sections. The target behavior is root-effective shell access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reverse Shell Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes multiple reverse shell payloads and cron callback examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples initiate shell callbacks to attacker-controlled placeholders. That is a clear remote-control pattern.

content.value_statement: Linux privilege escalation reviews require careful enumeration and clear evidence tracking. This skill organizes authorized assessment steps and remediation notes for security teams.
actual_capabilities: Enumerates Linux system, user, process, network, and filesystem details.
Identifies sudo, SUID, capability, cron, PATH, NFS, and kernel escalation paths.
Maps findings to known resources such as GTFOBins and exploit suggesters.
Documents exploitation evidence and remediation recommendations.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for common assessment blockers.
limitations: Requires explicit written authorization and a defined assessment scope.
Contains operational exploit steps that can damage systems or expose credentials.
Does not verify that a target belongs to the user or organization.
Does not replace controlled lab testing or human security oversight.

use_cases.title: Internal Linux Hardening Review
use_cases.description: Security engineers can inventory escalation paths and create prioritized hardening tasks.

use_cases.title: Authorized Penetration Test Documentation
use_cases.description: Penetration testers can structure evidence, scope notes, and remediation findings after approved testing.

use_cases.title: Security Training Lab
use_cases.description: Instructors can demonstrate common Linux misconfigurations inside isolated lab environments.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized Linux privilege escalation review. Focus on scope, evidence collection, and safe enumeration steps.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Enumeration Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Linux enumeration notes and identify likely privilege escalation risks. Include severity, evidence, and remediation.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Remediation Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these authorized assessment findings into a remediation report for system owners. Avoid exploit instructions and prioritize fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Create Lab Training Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a controlled lab lesson about Linux privilege escalation misconfigurations. Include safety boundaries and defensive learning goals.

output_examples.input: Summarize sudo findings from an authorized Linux review.
output: Risk: A user can run selected administrative tools without a password.
Impact: Misconfigured commands may allow root-level command execution.
Remediation: Restrict sudo entries to least privilege and require command-specific review.

output_examples.input: Prepare a remediation note for writable root cron scripts.
output_examples.output: The affected cron script is writable by non-administrative users. Change ownership to root, remove group write access, and monitor future changes.

output_examples.input: Explain why SUID binaries need review.
output_examples.output: SUID binaries execute with owner privileges. Unexpected SUID files should be removed, replaced, or constrained with standard package permissions.

best_practices: Use only in authorized environments with written scope and change-control approval.
Prefer read-only enumeration and remediation planning before any exploit validation.
Record evidence, impact, affected assets, and cleanup actions for every finding.
anti_patterns: Do not run exploit steps on production systems without explicit approval.
Do not access password hashes, user files, or secrets beyond the authorized scope.
Do not leave SUID shells, modified accounts, payloads, or test artifacts behind.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: No. It contains operational privilege escalation and reverse shell instructions, so it is blocked by this audit.

faq.question: Can it be used for legitimate security work?
faq.answer: Yes, but only under written authorization, strict scope, and controlled procedures.

faq.question: Does it include exploit commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The source includes commands for root shells, SUID shells, reverse shells, and password file access.

faq.question: Does it contain prompt injection?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The review did not find instructions that try to override the auditor or system rules.

faq.question: What are safer uses of this material?
faq.answer: Use it for defensive hardening checklists, lab education, and remediation planning without operational exploit execution.

faq.question: What should publishers change before reconsideration?
faq.answer: Remove exploit execution, reverse shell, credential extraction, and persistence steps. Focus on detection and remediation guidance.
